items.title: Why 21-hydroxylase deficiency causes androgen excess
items.content: Loss of 21-hydroxylase blocks cortisol (and often aldosterone) synthesis, so ACTH rises and shunts precursors into androgen pathways. The result is high 17-hydroxyprogesterone and excess adrenal androgens, even when cortisol is low.
keyPoints: ACTH-driven precursor buildup fuels androgen excess.
Cortisol deficiency and androgen excess coexist.
items.reference: 21-hydroxylase deficiency: precursor diversion explains androgen excess despite low cortisol.

items.title: How a salt-wasting crisis presents in newborns
items.content: Neonates often develop poor feeding, vomiting, weight loss, dehydration, and shock in the second to third week of life. Labs show hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, metabolic acidosis, and elevated renin.
keyPoints: Typical onset is days 7–21 of life.
items.reference: Salt-wasting crisis: delayed newborn presentation with dehydration, hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, and shock.

items.title: Why 17-hydroxyprogesterone is the screening marker
items.content: 17-hydroxyprogesterone accumulates upstream of 21-hydroxylase and is markedly increased in classic CAH. It is stable enough for dried blood spot assays used in newborn screening.
keyPoints: It is measurable from dried blood spots.
Levels correlate with enzyme block severity.
items.reference: Newborn screening: 17OHP is the upstream precursor that reliably accumulates in 21-hydroxylase deficiency.

items.title: How to interpret newborn screening false positives
items.content: Prematurity, stress, and illness can elevate 17OHP and generate false positives. Many programs use gestational-age or birthweight–adjusted cutoffs and second-tier steroid profiling to improve specificity.
items.reference: Newborn screening: prematurity and stress elevate 17OHP; adjusted cutoffs and second-tier assays help confirm.

items.title: Why 46,XX infants can have virilized genitalia
items.content: Excess fetal adrenal androgens masculinize external genitalia even with normal ovaries and uterus. The degree of virilization ranges from clitoromegaly to posterior fusion and a urogenital sinus.
items.reference: Virilization: prenatal androgen excess masculinizes external genitalia in 46,XX infants.

items.title: How Prader staging guides genital exam documentation
items.content: Prader staging grades external virilization from mild clitoromegaly to phallic structure with fused labioscrotal folds. Standardized staging improves communication and avoids subjective descriptors.
keyPoints: Describe phallic length, fusion, and urogenital opening.
items.reference: Prader staging: structured documentation improves consistency in describing virilized genitalia.

items.title: How to distinguish salt-wasting from simple virilizing CAH
items.content: Salt-wasting disease shows hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, dehydration, and high plasma renin activity. Simple virilizing CAH lacks mineralocorticoid crisis and typically maintains normal electrolytes.
items.reference: Phenotype differentiation: renin and electrolyte status identify salt-wasting versus simple virilizing CAH.

items.title: Why hydrocortisone is preferred in children
items.content: Hydrocortisone has a short half-life and lower growth suppression risk than longer-acting glucocorticoids. It allows physiologic replacement with flexible stress dosing.
keyPoints: Allows physiologic replacement and stress dosing.
Avoids overtreatment seen with long-acting agents.
items.reference: Glucocorticoid choice: hydrocortisone balances replacement with growth preservation in children.

items.title: How fludrocortisone and salt supplementation are used
items.content: Infants with salt-wasting CAH need fludrocortisone plus oral salt to maintain sodium balance and suppress renin. Doses are higher in early infancy due to renal resistance to mineralocorticoids.
keyPoints: Salt supplementation is essential in infancy.
items.reference: Mineralocorticoid replacement: fludrocortisone and salt support sodium balance and renin control in infancy.

items.title: Why growth and blood pressure are core monitoring targets
items.content: Overtreatment slows growth and causes Cushingoid features, while undertreatment accelerates growth with early epiphyseal fusion. Mineralocorticoid excess raises blood pressure, so BP and growth velocity must be tracked.
items.reference: Monitoring: growth velocity and blood pressure detect over- or under-replacement.

items.title: How to interpret 17OHP and androstenedione in follow-up
items.content: Values that are persistently high suggest undertreatment, while fully suppressed levels indicate overtreatment. Trends, timing of sample relative to dosing, and growth outcomes matter more than a single number.
keyPoints: Interpret labs in context of dosing and growth.
items.reference: Biochemical monitoring: 17OHP and androstenedione trends guide dose adjustments.

items.title: How stress dosing prevents adrenal crisis
items.content: Illness, surgery, or trauma requires increased hydrocortisone to mimic physiologic stress response. Families need clear sick-day rules and access to injectable hydrocortisone.
keyPoints: Stress doses mimic normal cortisol surge.
items.reference: Stress dosing: temporary hydrocortisone escalation prevents crisis during illness or surgery.

items.title: Why overtreatment can be as harmful as undertreatment
items.content: Excess glucocorticoid causes growth suppression, weight gain, hypertension, and iatrogenic Cushingoid appearance. It can also impair bone density and metabolic health.
keyPoints: Metabolic and bone effects can accumulate.
items.reference: Overtreatment: chronic glucocorticoid excess drives growth failure and metabolic complications.

items.title: How testicular adrenal rest tumors develop
items.content: Chronic ACTH stimulation can enlarge adrenal rest tissue within the testes, leading to mass effect and infertility. Routine exam and ultrasound during adolescence help detect TART early.
keyPoints: Screening is part of adolescent care.
items.reference: TART: prolonged ACTH stimulation promotes adrenal rest tumors with fertility impact.

items.title: Why prenatal dexamethasone remains controversial
items.content: Dexamethasone can reduce virilization if started very early in pregnancy, but most treated fetuses are unaffected carriers or males and receive unnecessary exposure. Long-term safety data are limited, so shared decision-making and research protocols are emphasized.
items.reference: Prenatal therapy: early dexamethasone may limit virilization but exposes many unaffected fetuses, so it is controversial.

items.title: How to counsel families about inheritance risk
items.content: Classic CAH is autosomal recessive; each pregnancy in carrier parents has a 25% risk of an affected child. Carrier testing and reproductive counseling help families plan future pregnancies.
items.reference: Inheritance counseling: autosomal recessive risk and carrier testing should be discussed with families.

items.title: Why 11β-hydroxylase deficiency causes hypertension
items.content: Accumulation of 11-deoxycorticosterone produces strong mineralocorticoid effects, leading to sodium retention and low renin hypertension despite low aldosterone.
items.reference: 11β-hydroxylase deficiency: DOC excess drives hypertension despite low aldosterone.

items.title: How 17α-hydroxylase deficiency affects puberty
items.content: Loss of sex steroid synthesis causes sexual infantilism with absent or delayed puberty and primary amenorrhea. Mineralocorticoid excess leads to hypertension and hypokalemia.
keyPoints: Sex steroid deficiency causes delayed puberty.
Androgen deficiency leads to undervirilization in 46,XY.
items.reference: 17α-hydroxylase deficiency: sex steroid deficiency delays puberty and causes hypertension.

items.title: Why P450 oxidoreductase deficiency can virilize mother and fetus
items.content: Impaired electron transfer reduces multiple steroidogenic enzymes, causing atypical androgen pathways. Maternal virilization can occur during pregnancy, and fetal genital ambiguity is common.
items.reference: POR deficiency: disrupted steroidogenesis can virilize both mother and fetus.

items.title: How the GIPP/GDPP checklist standardizes DSD assessment
items.content: A structured genital and phenotype checklist (GIPP/GDPP) ensures consistent documentation of gonadal, internal ductal, and external genital findings in suspected CAH. Standardization improves communication across the care team and supports shared decision-making.
keyPoints: Captures gonadal, ductal, and external findings.
items.reference: GIPP/GDPP: standardized DSD documentation improves clarity and multidisciplinary communication.

items.title: Why nonclassic CAH often presents in adolescence
items.content: Partial enzyme activity produces milder androgen excess that accumulates over time, leading to hirsutism, acne, irregular menses, or premature adrenarche rather than neonatal crisis.
keyPoints: Residual enzyme activity blunts neonatal presentation.
Symptoms mimic PCOS or premature adrenarche.
items.reference: Nonclassic CAH: partial enzyme activity delays symptoms to adolescence or adulthood.

items.title: How ACTH stimulation helps confirm equivocal cases
items.content: When baseline 17OHP is borderline, ACTH stimulation can reveal exaggerated precursor accumulation. A marked rise supports the diagnosis of CAH or nonclassic CAH.
keyPoints: A large rise indicates enzyme block.
items.reference: Diagnostic testing: ACTH stimulation unmasking of high 17OHP supports CAH diagnosis.

items.title: How to manage suspected adrenal crisis in CAH
items.content: Treat immediately with IV hydrocortisone, isotonic fluids, and glucose, without waiting for confirmatory labs. Correct electrolytes and continue mineralocorticoid replacement once stable.
keyPoints: IV hydrocortisone and fluids are first-line.
items.reference: Adrenal crisis: prompt IV hydrocortisone and fluid resuscitation are life-saving.

items.title: Why renin is high in mineralocorticoid deficiency
items.content: Loss of aldosterone leads to sodium wasting and volume depletion, activating the renin-angiotensin system. Persistent renin elevation indicates under-replacement with mineralocorticoid.
items.reference: Renin monitoring: elevated renin reflects inadequate mineralocorticoid replacement.

items.title: How to distinguish CAH from androgen-secreting tumors
items.content: Tumors usually cause rapid virilization and very high DHEA-S or testosterone without the classic 17OHP pattern. Imaging and tumor markers help when the androgen rise is abrupt or asymmetric.
keyPoints: Tumors show rapid progression and high DHEA-S.
Imaging is needed when growth is abrupt.
items.reference: Differential diagnosis: rapid androgen excess with high DHEA-S suggests a tumor rather than CAH.

items.title: Why fertility counseling is important in CAH
items.content: Chronic androgen excess, TART in males, and irregular ovulation in females can impair fertility. Optimized hormone control and reproductive support improve outcomes.
items.reference: Fertility: hormone control and TART screening are key to preserving reproductive potential.

items.title: Why sex assignment should be delayed until evaluation is complete
items.content: Immediate assignment in a virilized newborn can lead to misclassification. A careful evaluation of karyotype, internal anatomy, hormones, and family counseling helps align decisions with long-term outcomes.
items.reference: Sex assignment: delay until diagnostic evaluation and counseling are complete.

items.title: How long-term monitoring supports healthy transition to adult care
items.content: Transition planning addresses adherence, self-management, fertility counseling, and psychosocial support. Regular review of bone health, metabolic risk, and quality of life is required.
keyPoints: Psychosocial support is part of care.
items.reference: Transition care: structured follow-up supports long-term health and self-management.

items.question: A 12-day-old infant presents with vomiting, weight loss, and lethargy. Labs show Na 122 mEq/L, K 6.8 mEq/L, glucose 48 mg/dL. External genitalia are ambiguous. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: IV hydrocortisone and isotonic fluid resuscitation
items.explanation: This is an adrenal crisis; IV hydrocortisone and fluids are first-line and should not be delayed.
items.reference: Salt-wasting crisis: immediate IV hydrocortisone and fluids take priority over confirmatory testing.

items.question: A newborn screen returns markedly elevated 17-hydroxyprogesterone. Which diagnosis is most consistent with this pattern?
items.explanation: 17OHP accumulates upstream of 21-hydroxylase and is a classic screening marker.
items.reference: Screening: high 17OHP is a hallmark of 21-hydroxylase deficiency.

items.question: A 46,XX infant has clitoromegaly and partial labial fusion. Ultrasound shows a uterus. Which mechanism explains the external findings?
options: Maternal diabetes causing transient androgen excess
Fetal androgen excess from adrenal steroid precursors
items.explanation: Prenatal androgen excess from CAH masculinizes the external genitalia despite normal internal female organs.
items.reference: Virilization: fetal adrenal androgen excess drives external masculinization in 46,XX CAH.

items.question: Which glucocorticoid is preferred for routine replacement therapy in young children with CAH?
items.explanation: Hydrocortisone is short-acting and minimizes growth suppression.
items.reference: Therapy: hydrocortisone is preferred in children to reduce growth suppression.

items.question: A child with CAH has poor growth velocity, weight gain, and suppressed 17OHP. What is the most likely cause?
items.explanation: Suppressed 17OHP with Cushingoid features suggests glucocorticoid overtreatment.
items.reference: Monitoring: suppressed 17OHP and poor growth suggest overtreatment.

items.question: An infant with classic salt-wasting CAH continues to have high plasma renin activity despite hydrocortisone therapy. What is the next best step?
options: Increase fludrocortisone and ensure adequate salt intake
items.explanation: High renin indicates inadequate mineralocorticoid replacement; increase fludrocortisone and salt.
items.reference: Mineralocorticoid replacement: persistently high renin signals under-replacement.

items.question: A 15-year-old girl has hirsutism, acne, and irregular menses. Baseline 17OHP is mildly elevated. What test best confirms nonclassic CAH?
options: Low-dose ACTH challenge with cortisol only
items.explanation: ACTH stimulation demonstrating exaggerated 17OHP rise supports NCCAH.
items.reference: Nonclassic CAH: ACTH stimulation with elevated 17OHP confirms diagnosis.

items.question: Which clinical feature most strongly suggests 11β-hydroxylase deficiency rather than 21-hydroxylase deficiency?
items.explanation: DOC excess causes mineralocorticoid effects and hypertension.
items.reference: 11β-hydroxylase deficiency: DOC-driven hypertension differentiates it from 21-hydroxylase deficiency.

items.question: A 17-year-old phenotypic female has primary amenorrhea, no breast development, hypertension, and hypokalemia. Karyotype is 46,XY. Which enzyme defect is most likely?
items.explanation: 17α-hydroxylase deficiency causes sexual infantilism and mineralocorticoid excess.
items.reference: 17α-hydroxylase deficiency: sexual infantilism with hypertension fits the classic phenotype.

items.question: A pregnant woman develops virilization, and her newborn has ambiguous genitalia with low cortisol. Which disorder best explains both findings?
items.explanation: POR deficiency disrupts multiple steroidogenic enzymes and can virilize both mother and fetus.
items.reference: POR deficiency: combined maternal and fetal virilization with cortisol deficiency is characteristic.

items.question: Which statement best explains the controversy around prenatal dexamethasone for CAH?
options: It is ineffective at preventing virilization
Most exposed fetuses are unaffected carriers or males
It consistently causes congenital heart disease
It is only beneficial if started after 20 weeks
items.explanation: Early treatment may prevent virilization but exposes many unaffected fetuses to steroid.
items.reference: Prenatal therapy: most exposed fetuses are unaffected, raising risk-benefit concerns.

items.question: A 16-year-old boy with CAH has a firm testicular mass on exam. What is the most likely diagnosis?
items.explanation: Chronic ACTH stimulation can cause TART, often bilateral and associated with CAH.
items.reference: TART: adolescent males with CAH and testicular masses often have adrenal rest tumors.

items.question: Which factor most commonly contributes to false-positive 17OHP results on newborn screening?
items.explanation: Prematurity and stress elevate 17OHP, reducing screening specificity.
items.reference: Screening: prematurity and illness raise 17OHP and cause false positives.

items.question: A newborn has ambiguous genitalia and suspected CAH. Which is the most appropriate initial counseling message to parents?
options: Assign sex immediately to reduce anxiety
Delay sex assignment until evaluation and counseling are complete
Assure parents that long-term fertility will be normal
items.explanation: Delaying assignment allows accurate diagnosis and informed counseling.
items.reference: Sex assignment: defer until diagnostic workup and counseling are complete.

items.question: Which clinical feature is most typical of nonclassic 21-hydroxylase deficiency?
options: Hirsutism and irregular menses in adolescence
items.explanation: NCCAH presents later with androgen excess symptoms.
items.reference: Nonclassic CAH: adolescent hyperandrogenic symptoms are typical.

items.question: Which laboratory trend suggests undertreatment in a growing child with CAH?
options: Persistently elevated 17OHP and rapid growth velocity
items.explanation: High 17OHP with rapid growth indicates excessive androgen exposure and undertreatment.
items.reference: Monitoring: persistent 17OHP elevation with accelerated growth suggests undertreatment.

items.question: During a febrile illness, a child with CAH should receive stress dosing that is best described as:
options: Tripling the usual hydrocortisone dose until recovery
Stopping hydrocortisone to avoid adrenal suppression
items.explanation: Sick-day rules typically increase hydrocortisone two to threefold.
items.reference: Stress dosing: temporary hydrocortisone dose escalation during illness prevents crisis.

items.question: A 7-year-old girl develops rapidly progressive virilization over 2 months with very high DHEA-S. 17OHP is only mildly elevated. What is the most likely cause?
items.explanation: Rapid progression and very high DHEA-S point toward a tumor.
items.reference: Differential diagnosis: abrupt virilization with high DHEA-S suggests an adrenal tumor.

items.question: Two parents are known carriers of 21-hydroxylase deficiency. What is the probability that their next child will be affected?
items.explanation: Autosomal recessive inheritance yields a 25% affected risk per pregnancy.
items.reference: Inheritance: autosomal recessive disorders carry a 25% recurrence risk with two carriers.

items.question: Which lab finding most strongly supports adequate mineralocorticoid replacement in a salt-wasting infant?
items.explanation: Suppressed or normalized renin with stable electrolytes indicates adequate replacement.
items.reference: Mineralocorticoid monitoring: normalized or suppressed renin suggests adequate dosing.

items.question: In a newborn with suspected CAH and ambiguous genitalia, why is a standardized GIPP/GDPP documentation approach helpful?
options: It standardizes descriptions of gonadal, ductal, and genital findings
It determines final sex assignment immediately
It eliminates the need for hormone assays
items.explanation: Structured documentation improves communication and team decision-making.
items.reference: GIPP/GDPP: standardized DSD documentation clarifies gonadal, ductal, and genital findings.

items.question: A preterm infant has a mildly elevated 17OHP on screening. The next best step is:
options: Repeat 17OHP or perform second-tier steroid testing
Ignore the result as a false positive
items.explanation: Prematurity can cause false positives; repeat or second-tier testing improves specificity.
items.reference: Screening follow-up: repeat or second-tier testing clarifies false-positive elevations.

items.question: A 24-year-old woman with CAH wants to conceive. Which approach is most helpful for improving fertility?
options: Stop glucocorticoids to avoid fetal exposure
Optimize glucocorticoid therapy to control androgens
items.explanation: Androgen control improves ovulation and fertility outcomes.
items.reference: Fertility: optimizing androgen control supports ovulation and conception.

items.question: Which pattern best fits classic salt-wasting CAH due to 21-hydroxylase deficiency?
items.explanation: Classic salt-wasting CAH features high 17OHP with mineralocorticoid deficiency.
items.reference: Salt-wasting CAH: high 17OHP with elevated renin and hyponatremia is typical.

items.question: Which outcome is most likely in a child with chronic undertreatment of CAH?
options: Delayed bone age with short stature
Advanced bone age and early epiphyseal closure
No impact on growth or puberty
items.explanation: Excess androgens cause rapid growth and advanced bone age, limiting final height.
items.reference: Undertreatment: persistent androgen excess accelerates bone age and reduces adult height.

items.question: Which element is essential during transition of CAH care from pediatrics to adult services?
options: Review of emergency stress dosing and self-management skills
Switching to long-acting steroids for convenience only
items.explanation: Transition planning emphasizes self-management, including emergency dosing and adherence.
items.reference: Transition care: reinforcing stress-dose and self-management skills is essential.

items.statement: Prematurity can cause false-positive elevations of 17OHP on newborn screening.
items.explanation: Stress and prematurity elevate 17OHP levels.
items.reference: Screening: prematurity and stress can raise 17OHP and trigger false positives.

items.statement: Hyponatremia and hyperkalemia are expected in classic salt-wasting CAH.
items.explanation: Mineralocorticoid deficiency leads to sodium loss and potassium retention.
items.reference: Salt-wasting CAH: mineralocorticoid loss causes hyponatremia and hyperkalemia.

items.statement: Long-acting glucocorticoids are preferred over hydrocortisone in young children.
items.explanation: Hydrocortisone is preferred to minimize growth suppression.
items.reference: Therapy: hydrocortisone is preferred in children to reduce growth suppression.

items.statement: Persistently suppressed 17OHP suggests overtreatment with glucocorticoids.
items.explanation: Excess glucocorticoid suppresses ACTH and 17OHP production.
items.reference: Monitoring: suppressed 17OHP indicates possible overtreatment.

items.statement: Nonclassic CAH often presents with hirsutism or irregular menses rather than neonatal crisis.
items.explanation: Partial enzyme activity delays symptoms until adolescence or adulthood.
items.reference: Nonclassic CAH: mild enzyme defects present later with hyperandrogenic symptoms.

items.statement: 11β-hydroxylase deficiency typically causes hypertension due to DOC excess.
items.explanation: DOC has mineralocorticoid activity, raising blood pressure.
items.reference: 11β-hydroxylase deficiency: DOC excess drives hypertension.

items.statement: 17α-hydroxylase deficiency usually causes hypotension from salt loss.
items.explanation: It causes mineralocorticoid excess and hypertension.
items.reference: 17α-hydroxylase deficiency: mineralocorticoid excess causes hypertension.

items.statement: Testicular adrenal rest tumors can impair fertility in males with CAH.
items.explanation: TART can damage testicular tissue and obstruct sperm production.
items.reference: TART: adrenal rest tumors threaten male fertility if untreated.

items.statement: Prenatal dexamethasone therapy is universally recommended for all pregnancies at risk.
items.explanation: It is controversial due to exposure of unaffected fetuses and limited safety data.
items.reference: Prenatal therapy: universal treatment is not recommended due to risk-benefit concerns.

items.statement: Classic CAH due to 21-hydroxylase deficiency is autosomal recessive.
items.explanation: Two carrier parents have a 25% affected risk per pregnancy.
items.reference: Inheritance: 21-hydroxylase deficiency is autosomal recessive.

items.statement: Deferring sex assignment until diagnostic evaluation is complete is recommended in ambiguous genitalia.
items.explanation: This avoids premature decisions and supports informed counseling.
items.reference: Sex assignment: delay until evaluation and counseling are complete.

items.statement: Plasma renin activity is a useful marker to adjust mineralocorticoid dosing.
items.explanation: Renin reflects adequacy of sodium balance and mineralocorticoid effect.
items.reference: Mineralocorticoid monitoring: renin guides dosing adjustments.

items.assertion: Classic 21-hydroxylase deficiency causes elevated ACTH levels.
items.reason: Cortisol deficiency removes negative feedback to the pituitary.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the assertion.
items.reference: Pathophysiology: cortisol deficiency raises ACTH due to loss of negative feedback.

items.assertion: Infants with salt-wasting CAH often present in the second to third week of life.
items.reason: The initial neonatal mineralocorticoid state masks aldosterone deficiency for several weeks.
items.explanation: The timing is true, but the stated reason is not the main explanation; symptoms emerge as feeding increases and reserves are lost.
items.reference: Salt-wasting crisis: typical presentation is in the second to third week of life.

items.assertion: Hydrocortisone is the preferred glucocorticoid in children with CAH.
items.reason: Its short half-life reduces growth suppression compared with long-acting steroids.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the assertion.
items.reference: Therapy: hydrocortisone is preferred because it minimizes growth suppression.

items.reason: Excess glucocorticoid suppresses ACTH-driven steroid production.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the assertion.
items.reference: Monitoring: suppressed 17OHP reflects ACTH suppression from overtreatment.

items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the assertion.
items.reference: 11β-hydroxylase deficiency: DOC excess causes hypertension.

items.reason: Androgen and estrogen synthesis are reduced.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the assertion.
items.reference: 17α-hydroxylase deficiency: sex steroid deficiency leads to delayed puberty.

items.assertion: Nonclassic CAH can present with hirsutism in adolescence.
items.reason: Partial enzyme activity allows mild androgen excess over time.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the assertion.
items.reference: Nonclassic CAH: partial enzyme activity leads to gradual androgen excess.

items.assertion: Prenatal dexamethasone can reduce virilization in affected female fetuses.
items.reason: It suppresses fetal ACTH and adrenal androgen production when started early.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the assertion.
items.reference: Prenatal therapy: early dexamethasone suppresses fetal adrenal androgen production.

items.assertion: Fludrocortisone is used in salt-wasting CAH.
items.reason: Mineralocorticoid deficiency leads to sodium wasting and high renin.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the assertion.
items.reference: Mineralocorticoid replacement: fludrocortisone treats sodium wasting and high renin.

items.assertion: Testicular adrenal rest tumors can be detected during adolescence.
items.reason: They arise from ectopic adrenal tissue stimulated by chronic ACTH.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the assertion.
items.reference: TART: chronic ACTH stimulation leads to adrenal rest growth detectable in adolescence.

items.assertion: Prematurity can increase 17OHP levels on newborn screening.
items.reason: Premature infants have higher baseline adrenal steroid production.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the elevation is multifactorial and not solely due to baseline production.
items.reference: Screening: prematurity and stress elevate 17OHP and can cause false positives.

items.assertion: Sex assignment should be delayed in a newborn with ambiguous genitalia.
items.reason: Immediate assignment reduces the need for multidisciplinary evaluation.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false; evaluation is still required.
items.reference: Sex assignment: delay until evaluation and counseling are complete.
